county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Haskell County formed 1887 from Finney County. County seat is Sublette. Named for Dudley C. Haskell, US Representative from Kansas. Southwestern Kansas agricultural county. Dust Bowl effects were severe. Natural gas production. Sparsely populated.
county_metadata.research_tips: Haskell County Register of Deeds in Sublette holds land records from 1887. Formed from Finney County -- check Finney County records for earlier entries. A sparsely populated southwestern Kansas county. The Haskell County Historical Society has local family files. BLM GLO land patents are critical for tracing homesteaders. Probate Court records from 1887. Church records from pioneer congregations. Sublette newspapers at KSHS. FamilySearch has limited microfilmed records. Dust Bowl effects were severe in this county. Natural gas development brought later families. Mexican American agricultural workers settled from the mid-20th century.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: BLM GLO federal land patents for Haskell County; county deed books at the Register of Deeds office in Sublette

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Hodgeman County formed 1879 from Ford County. County seat is Jetmore. Named for Captain Amos Hodgeman, killed in Civil War. Western Kansas ranching and farming county. Dust Bowl effects caused outmigration.
county_metadata.research_tips: Hodgeman County Register of Deeds in Jetmore holds land records from 1879. Formed from Ford County. The Hodgeman County Historical Society has local collections. BLM GLO land patents document homestead entries. Probate Court records from 1879. Church records from Methodist and Catholic pioneer congregations. Jetmore newspapers at KSHS. FamilySearch has limited microfilmed records. The county experienced Dust Bowl outmigration. Cattle ranching records may supplement genealogical research; check KSHS manuscript collections. The county is named for Amos Hodgeman, a Civil War soldier.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: BLM GLO federal land patents for Hodgeman County; county deed books at the Register of Deeds office in Jetmore

county_metadata.parent_county: Original (unorganized territory -- originally Calhoun County, renamed 1859)
county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Jackson County formed 1855 as Calhoun County (renamed Jackson 1859). County seat is Holton. Named for Andrew Jackson. Prairie Band Potawatomi Reservation is within the county. Underground Railroad stations. Early territorial settlement.
county_metadata.research_tips: Jackson County (originally Calhoun County until 1859) Register of Deeds in Holton holds land records from 1855. The county includes the Prairie Band Potawatomi Reservation -- tribal records at NARA Kansas City and the Prairie Band Potawatomi Nation's offices. The Jackson County Historical Society in Holton has local collections. BLM GLO land patents. Church records from Methodist and Catholic congregations from the 1850s. Probate Court records from 1855. Holton Recorder newspaper at KSHS. FamilySearch has microfilmed Jackson County records. When searching early records, use 'Calhoun County' for the pre-1859 period. The Underground Railroad had stations in this county.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: BLM GLO federal land patents for Jackson County; county deed books at the Register of Deeds office in Holton

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Jefferson County formed 1855 as an original Kansas Territory county. County seat is Oskaloosa. Named for Thomas Jefferson. Free State movement stronghold. John Brown connections. Perry Lake constructed in the 1960s displaced some communities.
county_metadata.research_tips: Jefferson County Register of Deeds in Oskaloosa holds land records from 1855. One of the earliest settled Kansas counties in the Free State movement corridor. Oskaloosa and other towns were abolitionist strongholds. The Jefferson County Historical Society has local genealogical collections. BLM GLO land patents. John Brown had connections to Jefferson County -- check KSHS manuscript collections. Church records from Methodist, Baptist, and Quaker congregations from the 1850s. Probate Court records from 1855. Oskaloosa Independent newspaper at KSHS. FamilySearch has microfilmed Jefferson County records. Perry Lake construction displaced some communities.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: BLM GLO federal land patents for Jefferson County; county deed books at the Register of Deeds office in Oskaloosa

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Jewell County formed 1867, organized 1872. County seat is Mankato. Named for Colonel Lewis R. Jewell, killed in Civil War. Northern Kansas county bordering Nebraska. Republican River valley. Czech, Scandinavian, and German settlers.
county_metadata.research_tips: Jewell County Register of Deeds in Mankato holds land records from the 1870s. The Jewell County Historical Society has local collections. BLM GLO land patents document homestead entries. Probate Court records from the 1870s. Scandinavian, German, and Czech immigrant communities left church records from the 1870s-1880s. Mankato newspapers at KSHS. FamilySearch has microfilmed some Jewell County records. The county's northern border with Nebraska means cross-border family research may be necessary. The White Rock Creek valley was an early settlement area.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: BLM GLO federal land patents for Jewell County; county deed books at the Register of Deeds office in Mankato

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Johnson County formed 1855 as an original Kansas Territory county. County seat is Olathe. Named for Reverend Thomas Johnson, founder of the Shawnee Methodist Mission. Now the most populous Kansas county (Kansas City suburbs). Bleeding Kansas border war zone. Shawnee Indian Mission historic site.
county_metadata.research_tips: Johnson County Register of Deeds in Olathe holds land records from 1855. Now the most populous Kansas county (Kansas City suburbs). The Johnson County Museum of History in Shawnee has excellent local collections. The county was a Bleeding Kansas border war zone -- KSHS holds territorial-era records. Shawnee Indian Mission (Shawnee Methodist Mission) records at KSHS document Native American education. BLM GLO land patents. Strong Quaker community at Olathe left meeting records. Probate Court records from 1855. Olathe Mirror newspaper at KSHS. FamilySearch has microfilmed Johnson County records. District Court naturalization records at the courthouse. Suburban development since the 1950s means many modern records are at the county offices.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: Johnson County land records online index; historical deed books at county office

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Kearny County formed 1873, organized 1887. County seat is Lakin. Named for General Phil Kearny. Far-western Kansas county along the Arkansas River. Santa Fe Trail corridor. Late settlement. Irrigation agriculture.
county_metadata.research_tips: Kearny County Register of Deeds in Lakin holds land records from the 1880s. A far-western Kansas county along the Arkansas River. Named for General Phil Kearny. The Kearny County Historical Society and Museum in Lakin has local collections. BLM GLO land patents document homestead entries. Probate Court records from the 1880s. Church records from pioneer congregations. Lakin newspapers at KSHS. FamilySearch has limited microfilmed records. The Santa Fe Trail crossed through the county; check KSHS for trail-related records. Mexican American railroad workers settled along the Santa Fe line.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: BLM GLO federal land patents for Kearny County; county deed books at the Register of Deeds office in Lakin

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Kingman County formed 1872 from Reno County. County seat is Kingman. Named for Colonel Samuel A. Kingman, Kansas Chief Justice. South-central Kansas. German-Russian and Mennonite settlement. Oil and natural gas production.
county_metadata.research_tips: Kingman County Register of Deeds in Kingman holds land records from 1872. Formed from Reno County -- check Reno County records for earlier entries. The Kingman County Historical Society and Museum has local genealogical collections. BLM GLO land patents. German-Russian and Mennonite settlers left church records from the 1870s-1880s. Probate Court records from 1872. Kingman newspapers at KSHS. FamilySearch has microfilmed some Kingman County records. District Court naturalization records. The county produced oil and natural gas from the early 1900s. Cheney Reservoir construction affected some communities.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: BLM GLO federal land patents for Kingman County; county deed books at the Register of Deeds office in Kingman

county_metadata.record_losses: 2007 tornado destroyed significant portions of courthouse records; some older records survived in vault
county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Kiowa County formed 1886 from Comanche and Edwards counties. County seat is Greensburg. Named for the Kiowa people. The EF5 tornado of May 4, 2007, devastated Greensburg and damaged courthouse records. The town was rebuilt as a 'green' community. South-central Kansas ranching and farming county.
county_metadata.research_tips: Kiowa County courthouse in Greensburg was severely damaged by an EF5 tornado on May 4, 2007, which destroyed much of the town. Some older records survived in the courthouse vault, but significant losses occurred. The Register of Deeds has reconstructed records where possible. BLM GLO land patents provide backup for federal land entries. KSHS in Topeka may hold microfilm copies of pre-tornado records. FamilySearch microfilmed some Kiowa County records before the tornado. Check Comanche and Edwards county records for pre-1886 entries (parent counties). Greensburg newspapers at KSHS predate the tornado. The Big Well Museum (rebuilt) has some local history materials.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: BLM GLO federal land patents for Kiowa County; county deed books at the Register of Deeds office in Greensburg

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Labette County formed 1867 from Neosho County. County seat is Oswego. Named for a French trader named La Bette. Parsons was a major MKT Railroad town. The Bloody Benders serial murder case (1870s) is the county's most notorious historical event. African American Exoduster settlement.
county_metadata.research_tips: Labette County Register of Deeds in Oswego holds land records from 1867. Parsons was a major Missouri-Kansas-Texas (MKT) Railroad town -- railroad employee and land sale records at KSHS. The Labette County Historical Society and Museum in Oswego has local collections. BLM GLO land patents. The county attracted African American settlers (Exodusters) in the 1870s-1880s. Church records from Methodist, Baptist, and AME congregations. Probate Court records from 1867. Parsons and Oswego newspapers at KSHS. FamilySearch has microfilmed Labette County records. The Bloody Benders serial murder case (1870s) is documented at KSHS. District Court naturalization records.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: BLM GLO federal land patents for Labette County; county deed books at the Register of Deeds office in Oswego

source_profiles.source_name: Labette County Deed Books -- Register of Deeds
source_profiles.repository: Labette County Register of Deeds, Oswego
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Original deed books for Labette County from 1867; grantor/grantee indexes; mortgages; liens; Register of Deeds office maintains land records
source_profiles.survival_notes: Good survival from 1867; deed books are substantially complete

source_profiles.source_name: Labette County Probate Court Records
source_profiles.repository: Labette County Probate Court, Oswego
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Wills, administrations, inventories, guardianships, and estate settlements for Labette County; Probate Court has jurisdiction over estate matters

source_profiles.source_name: Labette County Federal Census Records
source_profiles.repository: NARA / FamilySearch / Ancestry
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Federal census 1870-1950 for Labette County; Kansas Territory census 1855 and 1859 may include Labette County entries at KSHS; state census 1865, 1875, 1885, 1895, 1905, 1915, 1925 at KSHS

source_profiles.source_name: Labette County Federal Land Patents -- BLM GLO
source_profiles.repository: Bureau of Land Management, General Land Office Records
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Federal land patents (homestead, preemption, timber culture, cash entry) for Labette County; Kansas is a public land state and GLO patents document original federal land transfers to private ownership

source_profiles.source_name: Labette County Territorial-Era Records -- KSHS
source_profiles.repository: Kansas State Historical Society, Topeka
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Kansas territorial records for Labette County including territorial census (1855, 1859), land claims, Free State and pro-slavery legislature records, correspondence, and early settler petitions held at KSHS

source_profiles.source_name: Labette County African American / Exoduster Records -- KSHS
source_profiles.repository: Kansas State Historical Society, Topeka / local AME churches
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Records documenting African American families in Labette County including Exoduster migration records (1870s-1880s), AME church records, freedmen's records, Colored Troops pension files, school records for segregated schools, and community organization records at KSHS

source_profiles.source_name: Labette County Marriage Records -- Probate Court / KSHS / FamilySearch
source_profiles.repository: Labette County Probate Court, Oswego / Kansas State Historical Society, Topeka
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Marriage licenses and records for Labette County from 1867; Probate Court issued marriage licenses in Kansas; KSHS holds some county marriage records on microfilm; FamilySearch has indexed Kansas marriages; state vital records registration began 1911
